raw_response: {"rating": -1.7, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. The author’s own framing tilts against Trump’s economic agenda by foregrounding a single critic’s negative assessment and offering no balancing perspectives, leading to a Lean Left rating. Key reasons include an editorial subhead that asserts the president’s bond-market threat is “worse” and causal narration linking market unease to Trump’s return to office. Detailed Analysis: (1) Editorial framing in a section header states, “Forget Trump’s tariffs, the president’s bond market threat is worse,” a direct, un-attributed judgment that negatively characterizes Trump’s actions; (2) The headline and deck center a single critic’s argument (“…says Stiglitz”) as the story’s frame, with no countervailing assessment from pro-administration or neutral economists featured in the author’s prose; (3) The author advances a causal narrative in their own voice: “The prospect of rising inflation… has led investors to pare back bets on the US Federal Reserve cutting interest rates since Trump’s return to office, amid mounting concern over the fallout from a global trade war,” attributing negative market shifts to Trump without presenting an alternative explanation; (4) Legitimizing language enhances the critic’s authority (“Nobel prize-winning,” “top economist”), while no comparable credibility signals are extended to any opposing view; (5) Sourcing is one-sided: the piece platforms criticism of Trump’s policies without including administration responses or supportive expert analysis, reinforcing a consistent anti-Trump tilt in the author’s framing. No quoted or platformed sources in the article are affiliated with extremist movements."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report. The author’s own framing tilts against Trump’s economic agenda by foregrounding a single critic’s negative assessment and offering no balancing perspectives, leading to a Lean Left rating. Key reasons include an editorial subhead that asserts the president’s bond-market threat is “worse” and causal narration linking market unease to Trump’s return to office. Detailed Analysis: (1) Editorial framing in a section header states, “Forget Trump’s tariffs, the president’s bond market threat is worse,” a direct, un-attributed judgment that negatively characterizes Trump’s actions; (2) The headline and deck center a single critic’s argument (“…says Stiglitz”) as the story’s frame, with no countervailing assessment from pro-administration or neutral economists featured in the author’s prose; (3) The author advances a causal narrative in their own voice: “The prospect of rising inflation… has led investors to pare back bets on the US Federal Reserve cutting interest rates since Trump’s return to office, amid mounting concern over the fallout from a global trade war,” attributing negative market shifts to Trump without presenting an alternative explanation; (4) Legitimizing language enhances the critic’s authority (“Nobel prize-winning,” “top economist”), while no comparable credibility signals are extended to any opposing view; (5) Sourcing is one-sided: the piece platforms criticism of Trump’s policies without including administration responses or supportive expert analysis, reinforcing a consistent anti-Trump tilt in the author’s framing. No quoted or platformed sources in the article are affiliated with extremist movements.

explanation: This is a news report. The author’s own framing tilts against Trump’s economic agenda by foregrounding a single critic’s negative assessment and offering no balancing perspectives, leading to a Lean Left rating. Key reasons include an editorial subhead that asserts the president’s bond-market threat is “worse” and causal narration linking market unease to Trump’s return to office. Detailed Analysis: (1) Editorial framing in a section header states, “Forget Trump’s tariffs, the president’s bond market threat is worse,” a direct, un-attributed judgment that negatively characterizes Trump’s actions; (2) The headline and deck center a single critic’s argument (“…says Stiglitz”) as the story’s frame, with no countervailing assessment from pro-administration or neutral economists featured in the author’s prose; (3) The author advances a causal narrative in their own voice: “The prospect of rising inflation… has led investors to pare back bets on the US Federal Reserve cutting interest rates since Trump’s return to office, amid mounting concern over the fallout from a global trade war,” attributing negative market shifts to Trump without presenting an alternative explanation; (4) Legitimizing language enhances the critic’s authority (“Nobel prize-winning,” “top economist”), while no comparable credibility signals are extended to any opposing view; (5) Sourcing is one-sided: the piece platforms criticism of Trump’s policies without including administration responses or supportive expert analysis, reinforcing a consistent anti-Trump tilt in the author’s framing. No quoted or platformed sources in the article are affiliated with extremist movements.
